region_of_origin: India (North Indian / Mughlai tradition); served in Singapore

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No exact FoodOn URI for Bharwan Tandoori Aloo; closest defensible match is stuffed potato dish (FOODON_03315091). Tandoor-grilled preparation and paneer/nut stuffing are not captured by this code.
ontology.foodex2_description: Potato-based dish — stuffed and grilled; FoodEx2 base term for potato dishes used as closest defensible anchor.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a specific code for tandoor-grilled stuffed potato. A0CKP (potato dish) used as the closest defensible anchor. Stuffing composition (paneer, nuts, spices) is not encoded.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code exists for Bharwan Tandoori Aloo or a sufficiently close stuffed potato preparation.

main_ingredients: spices (cumin, coriander, garam masala, turmeric, chilli)

serving.natural_language_portion: A standard serving of Bharwan Tandoori Aloo is approximately 250 g, equivalent to 3–4 stuffed potato halves as typically plated in Indian restaurants in Singapore.

nutrient_source.title: ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods (2017 edition) and USDA FoodData Central — component-based estimation for stuffed tandoori potato
nutrient_source.organization: National Institute of Nutrition (ICMR-NIN), India; United States Department of Agriculture (USDA)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single approved-source entry exists for Bharwan Tandoori Aloo as a composite dish. Nutrient values are estimated from ICMR-NIN data for boiled potato, paneer, and mixed nuts, cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central entries for paneer (FDC ID 2003590), roasted almonds (FDC ID 170567), and cashews (FDC ID 170162). The input record values (energy 180 kcal, protein 7.2 g, fat 11.2 g, carbohydrate 14.0 g) are consistent with this component-based estimation and are retained with medium confidence. Sodium 260 mg/100 g is plausible given marination with salt and spices.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson et al.; University of Sydney GI Database — boiled/baked potato analogous estimate
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database; American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Bharwan Tandoori Aloo. GI estimated at 65 (medium) based on the University of Sydney GI Database entry for boiled white potato (GI ~65–70), adjusted downward slightly for the protein and fat contribution of paneer and nuts, which slow gastric emptying and blunt the glycemic response. Carbohydrate per 100 g = 14.0 g; serving = 250 g; available carbohydrate per serving = 35.0 g; GL = (65 × 35.0) / 100 = 22.75, rounded to 22.8. This is a medium-GI, high-GL serving.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 260 mg/100 g; 650 mg per standard 250 g serving — approaching 28% of the 2,300 mg daily limit in one serving
hypertension.guidance: Patients with hypertension should limit portion size to 100–150 g and request reduced-salt marination, as a full 250 g serving delivers approximately 650 mg sodium; the potassium content (~800 mg per serving from potato and nuts) partially offsets the sodium load but does not eliminate the concern.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Medium GI (~65) with high glycemic load per serving (~22.8); potato is a high-GI base ingredient despite protein and fat buffering from paneer and nuts
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should limit serving size to 100–125 g to keep glycemic load below 12, pair with non-starchy vegetables or a high-fibre side, and avoid combining with other high-GI foods; the protein (7.2 g/100 g) and fat (11.2 g/100 g) from paneer and nuts help moderate the postprandial glucose response but do not eliminate the need for portion control.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat 4.0 g/100 g (10.0 g per 250 g serving) from paneer and yogurt; trans fat 0.08 g/100 g; sodium 260 mg/100 g
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Patients with cardiovascular disease should limit serving size to 100–150 g given the saturated fat contribution of 4–6 g per reduced serving; the MUFA and PUFA from almonds and cashews are cardioprotective, but the overall saturated fat and sodium load at full serving size warrants caution; tandoor grilling without added oil is preferable to pan-fried variants.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Potato is a nightshade vegetable (Solanaceae family); solanine content may aggravate joint inflammation in RA-sensitive individuals
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis who are sensitive to nightshade vegetables should avoid this dish, as potato is the primary ingredient and a significant source of solanine; anti-inflammatory components (turmeric, ginger, almonds, cashews, yogurt) are present but do not overcome the nightshade burden for sensitive patients.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Bharwan Tandoori Aloo (भरवां तंदूरी आलू) is a North Indian vegetarian dish consisting of whole or halved potatoes that are hollowed out and stuffed with a spiced mixture of paneer (Indian cottage cheese), chopped almonds, cashews, raisins, and aromatic spices including cumin, coriander, garam masala, turmeric, and green chilli. The stuffed potatoes are coated in a yogurt-based marinade seasoned with ginger, garlic, chilli powder, and chaat masala, then grilled in a traditional tandoor clay oven at high heat until the exterior is lightly charred and smoky while the interior remains tender. The dish delivers a complex flavour profile — earthy and smoky from the tandoor, creamy and rich from the paneer and nut stuffing, with a tangy yogurt marinade and warming spice notes. Texturally, the potato skin is slightly crisp while the filling is soft and dense. Nutritionally, per 100 g the dish provides approximately 180 kcal, 7.2 g protein (from paneer, almonds, and cashews), 11.2 g fat (4.0 g saturated from dairy), and 14.0 g carbohydrate with 2.0 g dietary fibre. Sodium is 260 mg/100 g, making portion control important for hypertensive individuals. The glycemic index is estimated at 65 (medium) based on the potato base, but the protein and fat content from paneer and nuts moderates the postprandial glucose response. A standard serving of 250 g (3–4 stuffed potato halves) delivers a glycemic load of approximately 22.8, which is high; individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should limit portions to 100–125 g. The dish is halal and vegetarian but contains dairy (paneer, yogurt) and tree nuts (almonds, cashews). It is commonly served as a starter or snack in Indian restaurants across Singapore and is associated with North Indian and Mughlai culinary traditions.
common_user_queries: Is Bharwan Aloo good for diabetics?
What is inside stuffed tandoori potato?
Can hypertension patients eat Bharwan Tandoori Aloo?

confidence_reason: No single approved-source entry exists for Bharwan Tandoori Aloo as a composite dish. Nutrient values are estimated from ICMR-NIN and USDA FoodData Central component data for potato, paneer, and mixed nuts. The input values are internally consistent and plausible for this dish type. GI is an analogous estimate based on boiled white potato from the University of Sydney GI Database. Medium confidence is appropriate given the component-based estimation methodology.
notes: 1. monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown for this composite tandoor-grilled stuffed potato dish is not available in ICMR-NIN or USDA FoodData Central as a single entry; component-level MUFA/PUFA data from almonds and cashews exist but cannot be reliably aggregated without exact recipe proportions. 2. vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1: micronutrient and moisture data for this specific composite preparation are not available as a single entry in ICMR-NIN or USDA FoodData Central; individual component data exist but cannot be reliably combined without exact recipe proportions. 3. potassium_mg (320 mg/100 g) is an estimate derived from ICMR-NIN potato and USDA nut component data; this value is not confirmed by a single-entry approved source. 4. The input source listed (ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods) is an approved primary source for Indian dishes and is retained as the primary nutrient reference. 5. The ethnic_tags in the input ('Butter Chicken', 'Curry', 'Biryani') are irrelevant to this dish and have been removed. 6. GI of 65 is an analogous estimate based on boiled white potato (University of Sydney GI Database); the actual GI of Bharwan Tandoori Aloo has not been directly measured in a peer-reviewed study. 7. Core macros (protein_g 7.2, fat_g 11.2, carbohydrate_g 14.0) are all non-zero and do not require zero-macro justification.
